input.new_string:
### Tasks
- `GET /api/projects/:id/tasks` — List tasks in a project (authenticated)
- `POST /api/projects/:id/tasks` — Create a task (authenticated)
- `PATCH /api/tasks/:id` — Update a task (authenticated)
- `DELETE /api/tasks/:id` — Delete a task (authenticated)

### Airtable Export
- `POST /api/projects/:id/exports` — Start an export to Airtable (admin/member only). Returns the initial snapshot. If an export is already in flight for the project, the existing one is returned instead.
- `GET /api/projects/:id/exports` — List recent export jobs for the project.
- `GET /api/projects/:id/exports/:exportId` — Status snapshot (Redis-first, DB fallback).
- `GET /api/projects/:id/exports/:exportId/errors` — Full per-record error list (cold DB read; called lazily by the UI).

## Airtable Export Setup

The export pushes every task in a project into a real Airtable base using the official `airtable` SDK. Idempotency is handled by Airtable's `performUpsert` keyed on a `TaskBoardId` field, so running the export multiple times never creates duplicates.

### 1. Configure environment

```env
AIRTABLE_API_KEY="pat..."           # personal access token
AIRTABLE_BASE_ID="app..."           # the Airtable base to write to
AIRTABLE_TABLE_NAME="Tasks"         # table within the base (default: Tasks)
REDIS_URL="redis://localhost:6379"
```

### 2. Required Airtable table schema

Create a table named `Tasks` (or whatever `AIRTABLE_TABLE_NAME` is set to) in your base with these fields:

| Field | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `TaskBoardId` | Single line text | **Upsert key** — must exist. |
| `Title` | Single line text | |
| `Description` | Long text | |
| `Status` | Single select | Options: `todo`, `in_progress`, `review`, `done` |
| `Assignee` | Single line text | Assignee's display name (nullable) |
| `CreatedAt` | Date | |
| `UpdatedAt` | Date | |

Missing fields surface as per-record `422`s in the export's `errors` list; they don't abort the job.

### 3. Run Redis and the worker

The worker is a long-running Node process (BullMQ). Redis is required.

```bash
# Docker (both services come up automatically)
docker compose up

# Or run the worker manually against your local Postgres/Redis
npm run worker          # production-style
npm run worker:dev      # with tsx watch
```

### 4. Trigger the export

Open a project's detail page as an admin or member and click **Export to Airtable**. Use the **Refresh** button on the status card to pull the latest progress — status reads hit Redis with a Postgres fallback, so refreshes stay cheap even under load. When the job is terminal you can re-trigger to run again; the upsert keeps Airtable in sync without duplicating records.

### How resilience is handled

- **Retry policy** — `429` / `5xx` / network errors are retried with exponential backoff (max 5 attempts). `401` / `403` / `404` mark the job `failed` immediately. `422` / `400` at the batch level falls back to per-record upserts so one bad row doesn't take down the other nine.
- **Crash recovery** — the worker writes a cursor (`lastProcessedTaskId`) and running counts to the `ExportJob` row after every batch. On worker restart or BullMQ stall detection, the job is re-picked-up and resumes from the cursor. Replayed batches are no-ops in Airtable thanks to `performUpsert`.
- **Single-flight per project** — `SET NX export:active:{projectId}` in Redis guarantees only one export runs at a time per project; concurrent POSTs return the already-running job.

## Tech Stack

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# TaskBoard — Project Management App

A Next.js 15 fullstack application for managing projects, tasks, and team members. TypeScript + Prisma + PostgreSQL on the server, React 19 + TanStack Query on the client.

## Quick Setup (Docker — Recommended)

```bash
# Clone and enter the repo
git clone <repo-url> && cd taskboard

# Start the app and database
docker-compose up --build

# In a separate terminal, set up the database
docker-compose exec web npm run db:seed

# Run the test suite
docker-compose exec web npm test

# The app is now running at http://localhost:3000
```

## Manual Setup (without Docker)

Requires: Node.js 20+, PostgreSQL 15+

```bash
# Run the setup script (installs deps, sets up DB, configures git hooks)
chmod +x bin/setup
./bin/setup

# Or do it manually:
npm install
git config core.hooksPath .git-hooks
cp .env.example .env   # then edit DATABASE_URL if your local Postgres differs
npx prisma migrate deploy
npx prisma generate
npm run db:seed
npm test
npm run dev
```

## AI Tool Conversation Tracking

**This repository is configured to automatically capture your AI coding tool conversation history with each git commit.** This includes conversations from Claude Code, Cursor, Aider, Continue.dev, Cody, Cline, and Windsurf.

This is part of the Ajackus evaluation process. We evaluate how you collaborate with AI tools — your prompting strategy, how you break down problems, and how you review AI suggestions. The captured conversations help us understand your workflow.

**How it works:**
- A pre-commit git hook runs automatically before each commit
- It copies conversation files from AI tool directories (e.g., `.claude/`, `.cursor/`) into `.ai-conversations/`
- These files are staged and included in your commit
- You don't need to do anything — it happens automatically

**What's captured:** Only AI tool conversation logs stored in the project directory. No system files, browsing history, or anything outside this repository.

**If you prefer a tool that doesn't store local conversations** (like browser-based ChatGPT), the screen recording will capture your interactions instead. No additional action needed from you.

## Seed Data

The seed file creates:
- 5 users across 3 projects with different roles (admin / member / viewer)
- 3 projects with realistic task distributions
- 12 tasks spanning all four statuses (`todo`, `in_progress`, `review`, `done`)

All user passwords are: `password123`

| Email | Role on which project |
|-------|----------------------|
| meera@taskboard.dev | admin on Q3 Launch & Internal Tools, member on Onboarding |
| arjun@taskboard.dev | admin on Onboarding, member on Q3 Launch |
| kavya@example.com | member on Q3 Launch |
| dev@example.com | viewer on Q3 Launch |
| lina@example.com | member on Onboarding |

## Authentication

Register or login to get a JWT token:

```bash
# Login
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/auth/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"email":"meera@taskboard.dev","password":"password123"}'

# Use the returned token
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" http://localhost:3000/api/projects
```

## API Endpoints

### Auth
- `POST /api/auth/register` — Create account
- `POST /api/auth/login` — Sign in, get JWT
- `GET /api/users/me` — Current user (authenticated)

### Projects
- `GET /api/projects` — List projects you're a member of (authenticated)
- `POST /api/projects` — Create a project (authenticated; creator becomes admin)
- `GET /api/projects/:id` — Project detail with tasks and members (authenticated)
- `PATCH /api/projects/:id` — Update project (authenticated)
- `DELETE /api/projects/:id` — Delete project (authenticated)

### Tasks
- `GET /api/projects/:id/tasks` — List tasks in a project (authenticated)
- `POST /api/projects/:id/tasks` — Create a task (authenticated)
- `PATCH /api/tasks/:id` — Update a task (authenticated)
- `DELETE /api/tasks/:id` — Delete a task (authenticated)

### Airtable Export
- `POST /api/projects/:id/exports` — Start an export to Airtable (admin/member only). Returns the initial snapshot. If an export is already in flight for the project, the existing one is returned instead.
- `GET /api/projects/:id/exports` — List recent export jobs for the project.
- `GET /api/projects/:id/exports/:exportId` — Status snapshot (Redis-first, DB fallback).
- `GET /api/projects/:id/exports/:exportId/errors` — Full per-record error list (cold DB read; called lazily by the UI).

## Airtable Export Setup

The export pushes every task in a project into a real Airtable base using the official `airtable` SDK. Idempotency is handled by Airtable's `performUpsert` keyed on a `TaskBoardId` field, so running the export multiple times never creates duplicates.

### 1. Configure environment

```env
AIRTABLE_API_KEY="pat..."           # personal access token
AIRTABLE_BASE_ID="app..."           # the Airtable base to write to
AIRTABLE_TABLE_NAME="Tasks"         # table within the base (default: Tasks)
REDIS_URL="redis://localhost:6379"
```

### 2. Required Airtable table schema

Create a table named `Tasks` (or whatever `AIRTABLE_TABLE_NAME` is set to) in your base with these fields:

| Field | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `TaskBoardId` | Single line text | **Upsert key** — must exist. |
| `Title` | Single line text | |
| `Description` | Long text | |
| `Status` | Single select | Options: `todo`, `in_progress`, `review`, `done` |
| `Assignee` | Single line text | Assignee's display name (nullable) |
| `CreatedAt` | Date | |
| `UpdatedAt` | Date | |

Missing fields surface as per-record `422`s in the export's `errors` list; they don't abort the job.

### 3. Run Redis and the worker

The worker is a long-running Node process (BullMQ). Redis is required.

```bash
# Docker (both services come up automatically)
docker compose up

# Or run the worker manually against your local Postgres/Redis
npm run worker          # production-style
npm run worker:dev      # with tsx watch
```

### 4. Trigger the export

Open a project's detail page as an admin or member and click **Export to Airtable**. Use the **Refresh** button on the status card to pull the latest progress — status reads hit Redis with a Postgres fallback, so refreshes stay cheap even under load. When the job is terminal you can re-trigger to run again; the upsert keeps Airtable in sync without duplicating records.

### How resilience is handled

- **Retry policy** — `429` / `5xx` / network errors are retried with exponential backoff (max 5 attempts). `401` / `403` / `404` mark the job `failed` immediately. `422` / `400` at the batch level falls back to per-record upserts so one bad row doesn't take down the other nine.
- **Crash recovery** — the worker writes a cursor (`lastProcessedTaskId`) and running counts to the `ExportJob` row after every batch. On worker restart or BullMQ stall detection, the job is re-picked-up and resumes from the cursor. Replayed batches are no-ops in Airtable thanks to `performUpsert`.
- **Single-flight per project** — `SET NX export:active:{projectId}` in Redis guarantees only one export runs at a time per project; concurrent POSTs return the already-running job.

## Tech Stack

- Node.js 20 (runtime)
- Next.js 15 (App Router) / React 19
- TypeScript 5 (strict mode)
- Prisma 6 + PostgreSQL 16
- TanStack Query 5 (client data)
- Zod 3 (schema validation)
- Tailwind CSS 3
- bcryptjs + jsonwebtoken
- Vitest 2 (testing)

content.text:
Typecheck clean, 91/91 tests pass. Implementation complete.

## Summary of what was built

**Backend**
- [prisma/schema.prisma](prisma/schema.prisma) — `ExportJob` model with checkpoint fields (`lastProcessedTaskId`, `heartbeatAt`, counters, errors JSON). Migration applied as `20260513121817_add_export_jobs`.
- [src/lib/redis.ts](src/lib/redis.ts) — shared `ioredis` clients (one general, one for BullMQ).
- [src/lib/airtable.ts](src/lib/airtable.ts) — real Airtable client using `performUpsert` on `TaskBoardId`. Retry classifier: `429`/`5xx`/network retried 5× with jittered exponential backoff; `401`/`403`/`404` throw `UnrecoverableExportError`; `422`/`400` at the batch level fall back to per-record upserts so one bad row doesn't kill the others.
- [src/lib/export-queue.ts](src/lib/export-queue.ts) — BullMQ queue with `attempts: 5`, exponential backoff, dedupe via `jobId`.
- [src/lib/export-worker.ts](src/lib/export-worker.ts) — processor with atomic claim, cursor pagination (`PAGE_SIZE=100`, `BATCH_SIZE=10`), per-batch checkpoint + Redis write-through, terminal cleanup of the active key.
- [src/lib/export-status-cache.ts](src/lib/export-status-cache.ts) — `tryClaimActive` / `clearActive` / `setStatus` / `getStatus` / `buildSnapshot` with stalled-status derivation.
- [bin/worker.ts](bin/worker.ts) — long-running entrypoint with SIGTERM/SIGINT graceful shutdown.

**API**
- [src/app/api/projects/[id]/exports/route.ts](src/app/api/projects/[id]/exports/route.ts) — `POST` start (authz admin/member, single-flight via Redis `SET NX`); `GET` list.
- [src/app/api/projects/[id]/exports/[exportId]/route.ts](src/app/api/projects/[id]/exports/[exportId]/route.ts) — `GET` status, Redis-first with DB fallback + cache backfill.
- [src/app/api/projects/[id]/exports/[exportId]/errors/route.ts](src/app/api/projects/[id]/exports/[exportId]/errors/route.ts) — `GET` full errors (cold DB path).

**UI**
- [src/components/ExportToAirtableButton.tsx](src/components/ExportToAirtableButton.tsx) — button for admin/member, refresh-on-demand status card, lazy error expansion, "run again" after terminal.
- [src/app/projects/[id]/page.tsx](src/app/projects/[id]/page.tsx:11) — wired into the project detail header.

**Infra**
- [docker-compose.yml](docker-compose.yml) — added `redis:7-alpine` and a `worker` service.
- [package.json](package.json:18) — `worker` / `worker:dev` scripts; `bullmq` + `ioredis` deps.
- [.env.example](.env.example) — `REDIS_URL`.

**Tests** — 27 new (91 total)
- [src/tests/lib/airtable-retry.test.ts](src/tests/lib/airtable-retry.test.ts) — 12 tests covering transient retries, permanent abort, 422 per-record fallback.
- [src/tests/api/exports.test.ts](src/tests/api/exports.test.ts) — 11 tests covering auth, role gating, single-flight, Redis caching, 404 cross-project.
- [src/tests/lib/export-worker.test.ts](src/tests/lib/export-worker.test.ts) — 4 tests: happy path, idempotent re-run, crash-resume from cursor, atomic-claim no-op on terminal.

**Docs** — [README.md](README.md:104) now has Airtable schema, env vars, worker run instructions, and the resilience model.

how the worker will start an pick jobs form laste server restart

Good question — let me walk through exactly how resumption works, scenario by scenario.

## The three pieces of durable state

| Lives in | Survives what | Used for |
|---|---|---|
| **Postgres** `export_jobs` row | Anything short of a DB wipe | Cursor (`lastProcessedTaskId`), counts, status, attempts, heartbeat — the source of truth |
| **Redis** BullMQ queue keys | Process restarts (Redis itself persists via AOF/RDB) | Job inbox, lock ownership, stalled-job detection |
| **Redis** `export:status:{id}` + `export:active:{projectId}` | Process restarts | Read-cache + single-flight guard (rebuildable from DB on miss) |

The worker process itself is **stateless** — it carries no in-memory progress that isn't already in Postgres or Redis.

## What "starting the worker" actually does

`npm run worker` → [bin/worker.ts](bin/worker.ts) → `createExportWorker()` in [src/lib/export-worker.ts:171-189](src/lib/export-worker.ts#L171-L189). The `Worker` constructor immediately starts polling the BullMQ queue — there is no "scan the DB for orphans" pass written by us, because we lean on the queue's own machinery.

## Scenario A — worker process restart (the common case)

This happens whenever the worker dies (OOM, deploy, crash, you killing it).

1. **Before crash**: worker took job `ej_123` out of BullMQ `waiting`, moved it to `active`, acquired a Redis lock with `lockDuration: 30_000`. Wrote `lastProcessedTaskId = task_0029` after batch 3.
2. **Crash**: process exits. The lock in Redis is not renewed.
3. **BullMQ stall detector** runs every `stalledInterval: 30_000` (set in [src/lib/export-worker.ts:177-180](src/lib/export-worker.ts#L177-L180)). When it sees an `active` job whose lock has expired, it moves it back to `waiting` and increments the stall counter. This happens on whichever worker is up — it doesn't need to be the same instance.
4. **Worker restart**: `npm run worker` boots. BullMQ pulls `ej_123` from waiting.
5. **processExport** runs again with the same `exportJobId`:
   - The atomic claim at [src/lib/export-worker.ts:69-79](src/lib/export-worker.ts#L69-L79) uses `status: { in: ['queued', 'running'] }`, so a job already in `running` state is re-claimable (a terminal job is *not* — that's the safety against double-finishing).
   - It reads the row, finds `lastProcessedTaskId = task_0029`, sets `cursor` from it ([src/lib/export-worker.ts:103](src/lib/export-worker.ts#L103)).
   - Cursor pagination resumes from `task_0030`. Already-pushed records that get re-sent on a partially-checkpointed batch are no-ops in Airtable because `performUpsert` matches on `TaskBoardId`.

So the worker's "resume" logic is literally three lines: read `lastProcessedTaskId`, pass it as the Prisma cursor, let `performUpsert` absorb any overlap.

## Scenario B — web server (Next.js) restart only

Workers are a separate process. Nothing happens to in-flight jobs. The only user-visible effect: POSTs in-flight during the restart fail with connection reset and the user retries.

## Scenario C — full restart (`docker compose down/up`)

1. `SIGTERM` reaches the worker container. The signal handler in [bin/worker.ts:21-31](bin/worker.ts#L21-L31) calls `worker.close()`, which **waits for the current batch to finish** before returning. The batch's checkpoint is written. Then the process exits 0.
2. Redis shuts down. The `redis:7-alpine` image has AOF disabled by default but RDB snapshots enabled (`save 3600 1 300 100 60 10000`) — and we mount a volume `redisdata:/data` in [docker-compose.yml](docker-compose.yml), so on restart the queue state survives.
3. Postgres shuts down. Its volume `pgdata` preserves the `export_jobs` row.
4. `docker compose up`. Redis and Postgres come up first (`depends_on`). The worker container starts, calls `createExportWorker()`, BullMQ connects, sees jobs in `waiting`/`active` from before shutdown, runs the stall sweep after 30 s, and processes them with the resume path from Scenario A.

If a job was caught mid-batch by an `SIGKILL` rather than a graceful shutdown (e.g. force-kill, OOM-killer), the last batch wasn't checkpointed — same as Scenario A, the overlap is absorbed by `performUpsert`.

## Scenario D — Redis data loss (the gap)

If Redis loses its data (volume wiped, AOF/RDB never persisted, FLUSHALL run), the BullMQ queue is empty even though `export_jobs` in Postgres still says `status='running'`. There is **no BullMQ entry to pick up**, so a worker restart alone won't resurrect those rows. They sit there until something re-enqueues them.

I documented this defense in the plan (§7, "BullMQ loses the job") but didn't ship the corresponding sweeper. The fix is a small repeatable BullMQ job:

```ts
// src/lib/export-sweeper.ts (not yet built)
//   every 60s, finds export_jobs where:
//     status IN ('queued', 'running')
//     AND heartbeat_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '5 min'
//     AND attempts < 5
//   ...and calls enqueueExport(job.id) for each.
```

Want me to add it now? It is genuinely belt-and-braces for Redis-loss scenarios — without it, the system is fine for every restart scenario *except* Redis data wipe.

## TL;DR

- **Worker only**: BullMQ + stall detector → re-enqueue → atomic claim allows `running → running` → cursor resumes from `lastProcessedTaskId` → `performUpsert` absorbs overlap. **Automatic, no operator action.**
- **Web only**: jobs unaffected.
- **Everything**: same as worker-only, plus we rely on the Redis volume to keep the queue. If you want to survive Redis data loss too, add the sweeper.

FROM node:20-bookworm-slim

RUN apt-get update -qq && \
    apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends openssl ca-certificates && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

WORKDIR /app

COPY package.json package-lock.json* ./
RUN npm install

COPY . .

RUN npx prisma generate

RUN chmod +x bin/setup bin/docker-entrypoint .git-hooks/* 2>/dev/null || true

EXPOSE 3000

ENTRYPOINT ["bin/docker-entrypoint"]
CMD ["npm", "run", "dev"]

content.text: The `node_modules` named volume in compose overlays the image's `/app/node_modules`. It was populated from a build that predates the new deps, so `bullmq` / `ioredis` aren't in it. Fix: drop that one volume (keeping `pgdata` and `redisdata`) and let the next container start repopulate it from a fresh image build.

content.text:
All four services healthy. The fix:

- `docker compose down` stopped containers (data volumes preserved).
- `docker volume rm q-taskboard-assessment_node_modules` dropped only the stale `node_modules` volume.
- `docker compose build worker` rebuilt the image so its `/app/node_modules` includes `bullmq` + `ioredis`.
- `docker compose up -d` started everything; the named volume was recreated from the fresh image's `node_modules`.

**One-liner for next time you add a runtime dep:**
```
docker compose down && docker volume rm q-taskboard-assessment_node_modules && docker compose up --build -d
```

This keeps `pgdata` and `redisdata` intact while picking up new packages.

content.text:
Quick survey:

- The secret is **only in commit `115f307`** ("feat: implement export queue and worker…") at `docker-compose.yml:53`.
- Your current working tree is already clean — `cee89c0` switched to `${AIRTABLE_API_KEY:-}`.
- Nothing has reached the remote yet (the push was rejected wholesale).

So we just need to scrub `115f307` and force-push. Two important things before I touch history:

### 1. Treat the token as compromised

GitHub already saw the secret in the rejected push payload (their scanner ran on it). Anthropic/Claude logs also have it from our session. **Revoke `[REDACTED_AIRTABLE_PAT]…` in Airtable now** and issue a new PAT. Whatever I do to git history, that specific token must die.

### 2. Pick the rewrite approach

questions.question: How should I rewrite history to remove the secret from commit 115f307?

options.label: Surgical rebase --edit (Recommended)
options.description: git rebase --interactive on 115f307^, mark that commit as 'edit', replace the literal token in docker-compose.yml with ${AIRTABLE_API_KEY:-} (matches what cee89c0 already does), amend, continue. Only that one commit's tree changes. Subsequent commits replay cleanly because cee89c0's docker-compose change becomes a no-op for those lines.

options.label: git filter-repo --replace-text
options.description: Replace the literal token string with REDACTED across every commit that contains it. Faster on large histories; rewrites the SHAs of every commit from 115f307 forward. Requires git-filter-repo installed (will check).

options.label: Unblock via GitHub URL
options.description: Use the URL GitHub gave (security/secret-scanning/unblock-secret/...) to let the secret through. NOT recommended — leaves the real token visible in your repo history forever, even after you revoke it. Only acceptable if the token is already revoked AND you don't mind it being grep-able forever.

services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: taskboard
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: taskboard
      POSTGRES_DB: taskboard
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    volumes:
      - redisdata:/data

  web:
    build: .
    command: npm run dev
    volumes:
      - .:/app
      - node_modules:/app/node_modules
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    depends_on:
      - db
      - redis
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://taskboard:taskboard@db:5432/taskboard?schema=public
      JWT_SECRET: dev-secret-change-me
      NODE_ENV: development
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379
      AIRTABLE_API_KEY: ${AIRTABLE_API_KEY:-}
      AIRTABLE_BASE_ID: ${AIRTABLE_BASE_ID:-}
      AIRTABLE_TABLE_NAME: ${AIRTABLE_TABLE_NAME:-Tasks}

  worker:
    build: .
    command: npm run worker
    volumes:
      - .:/app
      - node_modules:/app/node_modules
    depends_on:
      - db
      - redis
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://taskboard:taskboard@db:5432/taskboard?schema=public
      NODE_ENV: development
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379
      AIRTABLE_API_KEY: [REDACTED_AIRTABLE_PAT]
      AIRTABLE_BASE_ID: appmKc27rpAeTyMc7
      AIRTABLE_TABLE_NAME: Tasks

  test:
    build: .
    command: npm test
    volumes:
      - .:/app
      - node_modules:/app/node_modules
    depends_on:
      - db
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://taskboard:taskboard@db:5432/taskboard_test?schema=public
      JWT_SECRET: test-secret
      NODE_ENV: test

volumes:
  pgdata:
  redisdata:
  node_modules:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# Pre-commit hook: Automatically captures AI coding tool conversation logs.
# This is part of the Ajackus evaluation process and is disclosed in the README.
#
# Supported tools:
#   - Claude Code (.claude/)
#   - Cursor (.cursor/)
#   - Aider (.aider.chat.history.md, .aider.input.history, .aider/)
#   - Continue.dev (.continue/)
#   - Cody (.cody/)
#   - Cline (.cline/)

AI_LOG_DIR=".ai-conversations"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +"%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")

mkdir -p "$AI_LOG_DIR"

capture_if_exists() {
  local source="$1"
  local label="$2"

  if [ -e "$source" ]; then
    local dest="$AI_LOG_DIR/$label"
    mkdir -p "$dest"

    if [ -d "$source" ]; then
      cp -r "$source"/. "$dest/" 2>/dev/null
    else
      cp "$source" "$dest/" 2>/dev/null
    fi

    git add "$AI_LOG_DIR/$label" 2>/dev/null
  fi
}

# Claude Code — stores full conversation history as JSON
capture_if_exists ".claude" "claude-code"

# Cursor — stores workspace AI chat logs
capture_if_exists ".cursor" "cursor"
capture_if_exists ".cursorconversations" "cursor-conversations"

# Aider — stores full chat history as markdown
capture_if_exists ".aider.chat.history.md" "aider"
capture_if_exists ".aider.input.history" "aider"
capture_if_exists ".aider" "aider-sessions"

# Continue.dev — stores session data
capture_if_exists ".continue" "continue-dev"

# Cody (Sourcegraph) — stores chat history
capture_if_exists ".cody" "cody"

# Cline — stores conversation logs
capture_if_exists ".cline" "cline"

# Windsurf/Cascade — stores AI logs
capture_if_exists ".windsurf" "windsurf"

# Stage the AI log directory if anything was captured
if [ -d "$AI_LOG_DIR" ] && [ "$(ls -A "$AI_LOG_DIR" 2>/dev/null)" ]; then
  git add "$AI_LOG_DIR" 2>/dev/null
fi

exit 0

content.text:
Done. Summary of what's now in place:

- **`.claude`** in the repo root is a symlink → `~/.claude/projects/-Users-panchamsheoran-Desktop-q-taskboard-assessment/` (where Claude Code actually writes conversation `.jsonl` files).
- **`.gitignore`** ignores the symlink itself, so only its dereferenced contents end up tracked.
- **`core.hooksPath` = `.git-hooks`** — the existing pre-commit hook is now active.

On your next commit the hook will:
1. See `.claude` exists (via the symlink).
2. `cp -r .claude/. .ai-conversations/claude-code/` — follows the symlink and copies every `.jsonl` transcript (13 files right now, ~5 MB).
3. `git add .ai-conversations/claude-code/` so they land in the commit.

If you want to confirm before committing for real, you can run `bash .git-hooks/pre-commit` and then `git status` — it'll show the staged `.ai-conversations/claude-code/*.jsonl` files.

One heads-up: those JSONL files contain the **full conversation transcripts**, including any tokens/PATs you mentioned during the chat. Given we just spent time scrubbing the Airtable PAT out of git history, you'll want to check that the same token doesn't reappear inside a transcript file before pushing:

```bash
grep -l "[REDACTED_AIRTABLE_PAT]" .claude/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null
```

If it does, either revoke the token first (you should anyway), or selectively redact those JSONL lines before committing. Want me to scan them now?

content.text:
The token appears **20 times across 3 transcripts**:

| File | Matches | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `0ee1cc2e-…jsonl` | 1 | |
| `58864818-…jsonl` | 16 | Current session |
| `becf2b84-…jsonl` | 3 | |

If we commit these as-is, GitHub's push protection will reject the push again — same way it caught `docker-compose.yml`. No other obvious secrets (no `JWT_SECRET` leak, no plaintext passwords).

### Two ways to handle it

**Option A — redact in the hook** (recommended)
Patch `.git-hooks/pre-commit` to run a `sed` redaction pass over `.ai-conversations/` after copying. Local `~/.claude/projects/…` transcripts stay intact (full fidelity), only the committed copies get the token replaced with `[REDACTED_AIRTABLE_PAT]`. Pattern is generic enough to catch future Airtable PATs too (`pat<14chars>.<64hex>`).

**Option B — revoke first, commit raw**
You still need to revoke the PAT in Airtable regardless (it's leaked in Anthropic logs and GitHub's scanner). Once revoked, the string in the JSONL is just dead text — but GitHub's push protection scanner doesn't know that, so the push will still be blocked unless you click the unblock URL for each occurrence. Tedious.

I'd do A — surgical, idempotent across future commits, doesn't depend on a manual GitHub-side action. Want me to apply it?

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# Pre-commit hook: Automatically captures AI coding tool conversation logs.
# This is part of the Ajackus evaluation process and is disclosed in the README.
#
# Supported tools:
#   - Claude Code (.claude/)
#   - Cursor (.cursor/)
#   - Aider (.aider.chat.history.md, .aider.input.history, .aider/)
#   - Continue.dev (.continue/)
#   - Cody (.cody/)
#   - Cline (.cline/)

AI_LOG_DIR=".ai-conversations"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +"%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")

mkdir -p "$AI_LOG_DIR"

capture_if_exists() {
  local source="$1"
  local label="$2"

  if [ -e "$source" ]; then
    local dest="$AI_LOG_DIR/$label"
    mkdir -p "$dest"

    if [ -d "$source" ]; then
      cp -r "$source"/. "$dest/" 2>/dev/null
    else
      cp "$source" "$dest/" 2>/dev/null
    fi

    git add "$AI_LOG_DIR/$label" 2>/dev/null
  fi
}

# Claude Code — stores full conversation history as JSON
capture_if_exists ".claude" "claude-code"

# Cursor — stores workspace AI chat logs
capture_if_exists ".cursor" "cursor"
capture_if_exists ".cursorconversations" "cursor-conversations"

# Aider — stores full chat history as markdown
capture_if_exists ".aider.chat.history.md" "aider"
capture_if_exists ".aider.input.history" "aider"
capture_if_exists ".aider" "aider-sessions"

# Continue.dev — stores session data
capture_if_exists ".continue" "continue-dev"

# Cody (Sourcegraph) — stores chat history
capture_if_exists ".cody" "cody"

# Cline — stores conversation logs
capture_if_exists ".cline" "cline"

# Windsurf/Cascade — stores AI logs
capture_if_exists ".windsurf" "windsurf"

# Stage the AI log directory if anything was captured
if [ -d "$AI_LOG_DIR" ] && [ "$(ls -A "$AI_LOG_DIR" 2>/dev/null)" ]; then
  git add "$AI_LOG_DIR" 2>/dev/null
fi

exit 0

    | xargs -0 perl -pi -e '
        s/pat[A-Za-z0-9]{14,}\.[a-f0-9]{64}/[REDACTED_AIRTABLE_PAT]/g;

#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# Pre-commit hook: Automatically captures AI coding tool conversation logs.
# This is part of the Ajackus evaluation process and is disclosed in the README.
#
# Supported tools:
#   - Claude Code (.claude/)
#   - Cursor (.cursor/)
#   - Aider (.aider.chat.history.md, .aider.input.history, .aider/)
#   - Continue.dev (.continue/)
#   - Cody (.cody/)
#   - Cline (.cline/)

AI_LOG_DIR=".ai-conversations"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +"%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")

toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# Pre-commit hook: Automatically captures AI coding tool conversation logs.
# This is part of the Ajackus evaluation process and is disclosed in the README.
#
# Supported tools:
#   - Claude Code (.claude/)
#   - Cursor (.cursor/)
#   - Aider (.aider.chat.history.md, .aider.input.history, .aider/)
#   - Continue.dev (.continue/)
#   - Cody (.cody/)
#   - Cline (.cline/)

AI_LOG_DIR=".ai-conversations"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +"%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")

mkdir -p "$AI_LOG_DIR"

capture_if_exists() {
  local source="$1"
  local label="$2"

  if [ -e "$source" ]; then
    local dest="$AI_LOG_DIR/$label"
    mkdir -p "$dest"

    if [ -d "$source" ]; then
      cp -r "$source"/. "$dest/" 2>/dev/null
    else
      cp "$source" "$dest/" 2>/dev/null
    fi

    git add "$AI_LOG_DIR/$label" 2>/dev/null
  fi
}

# Claude Code — stores full conversation history as JSON
capture_if_exists ".claude" "claude-code"

# Cursor — stores workspace AI chat logs
capture_if_exists ".cursor" "cursor"
capture_if_exists ".cursorconversations" "cursor-conversations"

# Aider — stores full chat history as markdown
capture_if_exists ".aider.chat.history.md" "aider"
capture_if_exists ".aider.input.history" "aider"
capture_if_exists ".aider" "aider-sessions"

# Continue.dev — stores session data
capture_if_exists ".continue" "continue-dev"

# Cody (Sourcegraph) — stores chat history
capture_if_exists ".cody" "cody"

# Cline — stores conversation logs
capture_if_exists ".cline" "cline"

# Windsurf/Cascade — stores AI logs
capture_if_exists ".windsurf" "windsurf"

# Redact known secret patterns from captured transcripts before staging.
# Runs over every text file under $AI_LOG_DIR. Local source transcripts in
# ~/.claude/projects/... are untouched — only the committed copies are scrubbed.
# Add new patterns as needed; each is a perl-compatible s/// replacement.
if [ -d "$AI_LOG_DIR" ] && command -v perl >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  find "$AI_LOG_DIR" -type f \( -name '*.jsonl' -o -name '*.json' -o -name '*.md' -o -name '*.txt' \) -print0 \
    | xargs -0 perl -pi -e '
        s/pat[A-Za-z0-9]{14,}\.[a-f0-9]{64}/[REDACTED_AIRTABLE_PAT]/g;
        s/ghp_[A-Za-z0-9]{36,}/[REDACTED_GITHUB_PAT]/g;
        s/sk-(?:proj-)?[A-Za-z0-9_\-]{32,}/[REDACTED_OPENAI_KEY]/g;
        s/sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_\-]{32,}/[REDACTED_ANTHROPIC_KEY]/g;
        s/AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}/[REDACTED_AWS_ACCESS_KEY]/g;
      '
fi

# Stage the AI log directory if anything was captured
if [ -d "$AI_LOG_DIR" ] && [ "$(ls -A "$AI_LOG_DIR" 2>/dev/null)" ]; then
  git add "$AI_LOG_DIR" 2>/dev/null
fi

exit 0

content.text:
Hook is patched, all 31 occurrences redacted, the dry-run already staged the redacted JSONL files. Current state:

```
A  .ai-conversations/claude-code/*.jsonl   (12 transcripts, redacted)
 M .git-hooks/pre-commit                   (added redaction pass)
 M .gitignore                              (.claude symlink ignored)
```

A sample line confirms `[REDACTED_AIRTABLE_PAT]` substituted in — never `[REDACTED_AIRTABLE_PAT]` again.

### Next steps

1. **Revoke `[REDACTED_AIRTABLE_PAT]…` in Airtable.** Manual step in the UI. The string was leaked through Anthropic's prompt logs, GitHub's scanner cache, and a host of other places — even with the local repo scrubbed, the token must die.
2. **Commit + push.** When you're ready:
   ```
   git commit -m "feat: airtable export, transcript capture, secret-redacting hook"
   git push -u origin main
   ```
   The hook will re-run on the actual commit and re-redact in case any new content appeared in the live session file between now and then.

If you want to verify one more time before pushing:
```bash
git diff --staged | grep -c "[REDACTED_AIRTABLE_PAT]"   # should print 0
```

Future safety: the hook now catches Airtable / GitHub / OpenAI / Anthropic / AWS-access-key patterns generically, so a new leaked secret of any of those shapes gets scrubbed at commit time without any additional config.
